case_name: Johnson v. Advocate Health & Hospitals Corp.

summary.brief: Johnson v. Advocate Health & Hospitals Corp.. Medical malpractice case involving obstetrics gynaecology.

evidence_index.text: The plaintiffs argued that the child suffered brain damage from oxygen deprivation during the 45-minute period prior to delivery
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The hospital argued that the child’s injuries resulted solely from a condition that restricted his growth and development during the final weeks of pregnancy.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Following a three-week trial, a jury found Advocate Health and Hospitals Corporation (Advocate) liable for the neurodevelopmental disabilities of a child delivered at the hospital
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: AJ was small for his gestational age and Dr. Johnson suspected this was due to fetal growth restriction (FGR).
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: AJ’s baseline heart rate increased. A short time later, his heart rate dropped below the normal range for several minutes before returning to an elevated rate.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Johnson was incorrectly informed that AJ’s heart rate tracings were normal.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: AJ was pale. He was not alert, did not respond to stimuli, and was not moving or breathing on his own.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: AJ was placed in a cooling blanket to prevent brain cell deterioration.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: AJ was diagnosed with neonatal encephalopathy—brain dysfunction in a newborn.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The plaintiffs’ experts opined that the cause of AJ’s permanent disabilities was hypoxic ischemic encephalopathy (HIE)—brain damage resulting from lack of oxygen and low blood flow.
evidence_index.speaker: Plaintiffs' experts

evidence_index.text: We affirm the judgment of the circuit court.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: AJ was small for gestational age, suspected due to fetal growth restriction. Labor was induced with Pitocin.

focused_exam.general: Pale, not alert, no spontaneous movement or breathing at birth

critical_findings: High lactic acid and carbon dioxide levels

requestables.name: Blood Gas Analysis
requestables.description: Assessment of blood oxygen, carbon dioxide, and pH levels

reveal.result_summary: High lactic acid and carbon dioxide, low oxygen
reveal.decision_impact: Supports diagnosis of hypoxic ischemic encephalopathy

requestables.name: MRI Brain
requestables.description: Magnetic resonance imaging of the brain

reveal.detailed_findings: MRI indicated damage to brain areas furthest from blood flow, consistent with HIE
reveal.clinical_significance: Supports diagnosis of hypoxic ischemic encephalopathy
reveal.decision_impact: Confirms brain injury pattern typical of HIE

timeline_phases.name: Birth and Initial Assessment
timeline_phases.description: AJ was born and assessed for signs of distress.

timeline_phases.name: Neonatal Assessment and Management
timeline_phases.description: AJ was assessed for encephalopathy and management initiated.

decision_points.clinical_context: Fetal distress during labor with abnormal heart rate tracings
decision_points.prompt: What should be the next step in management?

options.clinical_reasoning: Urgent delivery needed due to fetal distress

options.description: Continue monitoring and delay Cesarean section
options.clinical_reasoning: Delay in response to fetal distress

actual_action_defendant.description: Delayed Cesarean section despite fetal distress

expected_action_court.description: Immediate Cesarean section should have been performed
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert testimony indicated urgency due to fetal distress

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Delay in Cesarean section led to prolonged hypoxia
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Immediate Cesarean section to prevent hypoxic injury
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Breach of duty due to failure to act on fetal distress

patient_outcome.description: AJ developed permanent disabilities due to hypoxic ischemic encephalopathy.

key_findings: Breach of duty in failing to provide accurate heart rate information.
Causation established - earlier intervention could have prevented some disabilities.

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to provide accurate heart rate information.
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Accurate information would have led to earlier intervention.

allegations.allegation: Failure to perform timely Cesarean section
Failure to provide accurate heart rate information

court_findings.legal_significance: Failure to act on fetal distress
Liability found for failure to provide accurate information
